skill.name: feedback-mastery
skill.description: Navigate difficult workplace conversations and deliver constructive feedback using structured frameworks, SBI examples, scripts, and follow-up checklists.
skill.summary: Structure workplace feedback and difficult conversations with SBI scripts, preparation steps, and follow-up guidance.
skill.author: softaworks

security_audit.summary: AI review found the static analyzer results to be false positives caused by markdown formatting, example dialogue, reference paths, and ordinary workplace scheduling language. No evidence found of executable code, shell commands, network access, credential handling, prompt injection, or malicious intent.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Text Flagged as External Commands
low_findings.description: Static command-execution detections occur in markdown examples, fenced text blocks, or inline reference paths. These are user-facing communication materials, not executable Ruby, shell, or command invocation logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced locations contain markdown examples, text fences, or resource links only. I found no executable command syntax, script file, command runner, or user input path.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Workplace Language Flagged as Security Blockers
low_findings.description: Static weak-crypto and reconnaissance detections are caused by normal prose in a workplace feedback guide. The lines discuss frameworks, conversations, schedules, examples, or word choices, not cryptography or system discovery.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review confirms these are ordinary markdown guide lines about feedback, expectations, and meetings. There are no cryptographic APIs, reconnaissance commands, or operational instructions at these locations.

content.value_statement: Difficult workplace conversations often fail because feedback is vague, emotional, or poorly timed. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured scripts, SBI examples, and follow-up plans for clear professional communication.
actual_capabilities: Structures feedback with the Situation-Behavior-Impact model.
Provides opening lines for performance, conflict, and expectation conversations.
Helps prepare before, during, and after difficult conversations.
Reframes accusatory language into specific, observable statements.
Creates follow-up messages, check-in plans, and expectation summaries.
Offers playbooks for missed deadlines, quality concerns, team conflict, and scope creep.
limitations: Does not replace HR, legal, medical, or compliance advice.
Requires accurate context from the user to produce fair feedback.
Does not decide employment actions or disciplinary outcomes.
May need adaptation for company policy, culture, and local law.

use_cases.title: Prepare Performance Feedback
use_cases.description: Turn vague concerns into specific SBI feedback with a calm opening, impact statement, and next steps.

use_cases.title: Resolve Peer Conflict
use_cases.description: Plan a neutral conversation that separates facts, perspectives, and options for moving forward.

use_cases.title: Reset Stakeholder Expectations
use_cases.description: Explain timeline, scope, or ownership changes with options, trade-offs, and written confirmation.

prompt_templates.title: Draft Simple SBI Feedback
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me write constructive SBI feedback. Situation: [specific event]. Behavior: [observable action]. Impact: [effect]. Keep the tone direct and respectful.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare a Difficult One-on-One
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me prepare for a difficult one-on-one about [issue]. Give me an opening line, key facts to mention, questions to ask, and a follow-up plan.

prompt_templates.title: Reframe Blunt Feedback
prompt_templates.prompt: Rewrite this feedback so it is specific, fair, and actionable: [rough feedback]. Use observable behavior, clear impact, and a collaborative next step.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Full Conversation Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a complete conversation plan for [scenario]. Include preparation questions, likely reactions, response scripts, trade-offs, agreement language, and follow-up messages.

output_examples.input: I need to tell a developer their last releases had too many bugs.
output: Opening: I want to talk about a pattern in the last few releases and understand what is getting in the way.
SBI: In the last two releases, several defects reached QA after review. This delayed validation and required extra support from the team.
Next step: Can we look at the test plan together and agree on what support would help before the next release?

output_examples.input: A stakeholder wants a feature in four weeks, but the estimate is eight weeks.
output: Acknowledge the goal and explain the delivery risk with comparable work or capacity data.
Offer options such as reduced scope, later delivery, or added support with onboarding time.
Close by asking which trade-off they approve and confirm it in writing.

output_examples.input: Two teammates are arguing about a technical decision.
output: Meet separately first to understand each perspective without asking either person to defend themselves.
In the joint discussion, name the shared goal and the specific decision point.
Agree on a decision process and check back after the team has used it.

best_practices: Use concrete examples and avoid labels about character or intent.
Prepare your desired outcome before choosing words for the conversation.
Document agreements, owners, dates, and support after the conversation.
anti_patterns: Using words like always or never instead of specific evidence.
Starting the conversation while angry or trying to win an argument.
Ending with vague agreement and no owner, timeline, or follow-up.

faq.question: What framework does this skill use for feedback?
faq.answer: It uses Situation-Behavior-Impact for feedback and a preparation, delivery, and follow-up model for conversations.

faq.question: Can it help with positive feedback?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for recognition, code reviews, collaboration, mentorship, and cross-team support.

faq.question: Can it write exact scripts?
faq.answer: Yes. It can draft opening lines, response phrases, follow-up messages, and expectation summaries.

faq.question: Is this only for software teams?
faq.answer: No. Many examples use software work, but the feedback and expectation frameworks apply to most professional teams.

faq.question: Does it make HR or legal decisions?
faq.answer: No. It supports communication planning and should be used with company policy and qualified advice when needed.

faq.question: How should I get the best result?
faq.answer: Provide the situation, observed behavior, impact, desired outcome, and any constraints before asking for a script.
